source: ILO Future of Work — Working Papers Series, 'Robots and the Risk of Job Automation in Developing Countries' (Carbonero et al., 2018) and ILO 'Changing Demand for Skills in Digital Economies and Societies' (2021). Task-composition adjustment factors for LMIC contexts vs. US baseline.
method: Multiplicative dampening factor applied to raw Frey-Osborne automation probability. Reflects: (a) lower capital intensity, (b) lower labour cost vs. automation cost, (c) higher share of non-routine manual tasks in LMIC informal-sector occupations. Adjusted probability = clamp(p_raw * factor, 0, 1).

GH.comment: Ghana — high informality (~86%), low robot density (~0/10k workers, ILO 2022), lower capital substitution feasibility. Applied across most occupations.

AM.comment: Armenia — moderate formality, strong IT sector (~28% 5yr ICT growth), gradually rising robot density and digital substitution.
